title: Cell Structure Basics For MDCAT
concept: Cell structure questions usually test organelles, their functions and the difference between prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells.
workedExample: If a question asks for the site of protein synthesis, connect the function to ribosomes. If it asks for ATP production, connect it to mitochondria.
strategy: Learn each organelle through function first, then location and structure. This prevents confusion in close MCQ options.
commonMistake: Students often memorise organelle names but cannot match them with functions under exam pressure.

title: Percentages For FAST And NAT
concept: Percentage questions compare change with the original value. The original value is the base.
workedExample: If 80 becomes 100, the increase is 20. Percentage increase = 20/80 x 100 = 25%.
strategy: Write original, change and final value before using the formula. This stops the common base-value mistake.
commonMistake: Using the final value as the denominator when the question asks for percentage increase from the original.

title: Assumptions In GAT Logical Reasoning
concept: An assumption is an unstated idea that must be true for the argument to work.
workedExample: If an argument says daily revision improves scores, it may assume that revision quality is similar across students.
strategy: Find the conclusion first, then ask what hidden idea connects the evidence to that conclusion.
commonMistake: Choosing an answer that sounds true but is not necessary for the argument.

title: Vocabulary In Context For GRE And SAT
concept: Context vocabulary depends on sentence logic, not only memorised word lists.
workedExample: In 'Although the task looked simple, it was arduous,' the contrast tells us arduous means difficult.
strategy: Mark contrast words, cause-effect signals and tone before selecting an option.
commonMistake: Choosing a familiar meaning without checking the sentence direction.
